The **Ospreys** ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *Y Gweilch*), formerly the Neath–Swansea Ospreys[[2]](#cite_note-walesonline1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) is one of the four professional men's [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") teams from [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"). They compete in the [United Rugby Championship](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship") and in [European Professional Club Rugby](/wiki/European_Professional_Club_Rugby "European Professional Club Rugby") competitions. The team formed as a result of [Neath RFC](/wiki/Neath_RFC "Neath RFC") and [Swansea RFC](/wiki/Swansea_RFC "Swansea RFC") combining to create a new merged entity, as part of the [new regional structure of Welsh rugby](/wiki/Introduction_of_regional_rugby_union_teams_in_Wales "Introduction of regional rugby union teams in Wales"), that began in 2003. They are also affiliated with a number of local semi-professional and amateur clubs, including Welsh Premier Division sides [Aberavon RFC](/wiki/Aberavon_RFC "Aberavon RFC"), [Bridgend Ravens](/wiki/Bridgend_Ravens "Bridgend Ravens"), and original founding clubs Neath and Swansea. The regional area represented by the team has widely become known for rugby purposes as 'Ospreylia'.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Their main home ground is [St Helen's](/wiki/St_Helen%27s_Rugby_and_Cricket_Ground "St Helen's Rugby and Cricket Ground"), Swansea. The Ospreys played for 20 years at [Swansea.com Stadium](/wiki/Swansea.com_Stadium "Swansea.com Stadium"). Some smaller profile games have been played at the [Brewery Field](/wiki/Brewery_Field "Brewery Field"), Bridgend. Ospreys currently play in a black home strip, while the away strip is white. The Ospreys logo consists of an image of an [Osprey](/wiki/Osprey "Osprey") mask.

The Ospreys are the most successful Welsh team in the history of the [Celtic League or Pro12](/wiki/Pro14 "Pro14") tournament, having won the competition four times. They also became the first and only Welsh regional team to beat a major touring side, defeating [Australia A](/wiki/Australia_A_national_rugby_union_team "Australia A national rugby union team") 24–16 in 2006.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ospreys_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

|  |
| --- |
| Cardiff Blues  Cardiff  Scarlets  Llanelli  Ospreys  Swansea  Neath  Dragons  Newport  The 4 Welsh Rugby Union Regions of Wales |

### Infancy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ospreys_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Infancy")]

In February 2003 an emergency meeting of clubs in the [Welsh Rugby Union](/wiki/Welsh_Rugby_Union "Welsh Rugby Union") voted to change its 130 year tradition and to devolve from local RFCs to regions.[[6]](#cite_note-6) On 24 July 2003, it was announced that the new team jointly representing [Neath RFC](/wiki/Neath_RFC "Neath RFC") and [Swansea RFC](/wiki/Swansea_RFC "Swansea RFC") would be known as the Neath–Swansea Ospreys.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The Ospreys part of the team's name was inspired by the use of the bird as Swansea RFC's centenary badge. Former Neath RFC coach [Lyn Jones](/wiki/Lyn_Jones "Lyn Jones") was appointed as head coach, who named [Scott Gibbs](/wiki/Scott_Gibbs "Scott Gibbs") as the team's first captain.[[8]](#cite_note-8) 5 September 2003 saw the region play their first competitive game, a 41–30 [Celtic League](/wiki/Pro14 "Pro14") home win over Irish province [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster_rugby "Ulster rugby") at [The Gnoll](/wiki/The_Gnoll "The Gnoll") in [Neath](/wiki/Neath "Neath").[[9]](#cite_note-9) [Gavin Henson](/wiki/Gavin_Henson "Gavin Henson") was a substitute in that game but scored two tries and a penalty helping the team to victory. [Leeds Tykes](/wiki/Leeds_Tykes "Leeds Tykes") were the Ospreys' first [Heineken Cup](/wiki/Heineken_Cup "Heineken Cup") opponents, with the English team triumphing 29–20 on 7 December 2003.[[10]](#cite_note-10) They struggled to recover from their opening defeat, finishing bottom of their pool, recording their only victory against the Tykes' at home in the final round at [St. Helens](/wiki/St._Helen%27s_Rugby_and_Cricket_Ground "St. Helen's Rugby and Cricket Ground").[[11]](#cite_note-11) Domestically the Ospreys' did salvage some pride, avoiding becoming the lowest placed Welsh region, by pipping [Cardiff Blues](/wiki/Cardiff_Blues "Cardiff Blues") to fifth place in the Celtic League.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Following the demise of the [Celtic Warriors](/wiki/Celtic_Warriors "Celtic Warriors") region on 1 June 2004,[[13]](#cite_note-13) the Neath–Swansea Ospreys had their borders extended to cover much of the [Bridgend](/wiki/Bridgend "Bridgend") and [Ogmore](/wiki/Ogmore-by-Sea "Ogmore-by-Sea") areas to the east.[[14]](#cite_note-14) However, for logistical reasons it was decided that no home games would be played at [Bridgend RFC](/wiki/Bridgend_RFC "Bridgend RFC")'s [Brewery Field](/wiki/Brewery_Field "Brewery Field").[[15]](#cite_note-15) Former Warriors players David Bishop, Brent Cockbain, Ryan Jones and Sonny Parker were signed by the Ospreys'.

### 2004–2007

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ospreys_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2004–2007")]

The 2004–05 season saw significant on field improvement for the region. A capacity crowd of 10,280 was present at The Gnoll on 26 March 2005 to witness the Ospreys claim their first piece of silverware. A 29–12 win over [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh_Rugby "Edinburgh Rugby"), with two rounds of the competition still remaining, saw the team crowned Celtic League champions.[[16]](#cite_note-16) However, impressive back to back wins over [Harlequins](/wiki/Harlequin_F.C. "Harlequin F.C.") were not enough to overcome [Munster](/wiki/Munster_Rugby "Munster Rugby") in the pool stages of [that season's Heineken Cup](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_Heineken_Cup "2004–05 Heineken Cup"). On 14 May 2005 it was announced that the "Neath–Swansea" part of the team's name would be dropped, with them to be referred to as the Ospreys.[[2]](#cite_note-walesonline1-2)

A growing sense of expectation surrounded the team heading into 2005–06, an expectation they struggled to cope with, finishing a disappointing seventh in the Celtic League. A difficult Heineken Cup pool made up of tough opposition in [Leicester Tigers](/wiki/Leicester_Tigers "Leicester Tigers"), [Stade Francais](/wiki/Stade_Francais "Stade Francais") and [ASM Clermont Auvergne](/wiki/ASM_Clermont_Auvergne "ASM Clermont Auvergne") proved too difficult, as they finished a distant third. A gutsy home victory over Stade[[17]](#cite_note-17) and a last gasp defeat to Leicester,[[18]](#cite_note-18) did however offer some crumbs of comfort. In April 2006 it was announced that noted former New Zealand [All Blacks](/wiki/All_Blacks "All Blacks") [scrum half](/wiki/Scrum-half_(rugby_union) "Scrum-half (rugby union)") [Justin Marshall](/wiki/Justin_Marshall "Justin Marshall") had signed to play for Ospreys.

The Ospreys won the [Celtic League](/wiki/Pro14 "Pro14") during the 2006/07 season, topping the league by a single point and taking the title with an away win at Borders. Winning the Celtic League for a second time meant the Ospreys were the first team to have won the Celtic League twice. In February 2008, the Ospreys provided 13 of the starting line-up for [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_union_team "Wales national rugby union team") in their [Six Nations](/wiki/Six_Nations_Championship "Six Nations Championship") match against England, setting a new record for the number of players from one region playing for their national side. During the 2007/08 season the Ospreys reached Quarter-final of the [Heineken Cup](/wiki/Heineken_Cup "Heineken Cup") for the first time but unexpectedly lost to [Saracens](/wiki/Saracens_F.C. "Saracens F.C."). The following week they won the [EDF Energy Cup](/wiki/EDF_Energy_Cup "EDF Energy Cup") beating [Leicester Tigers](/wiki/Leicester_Tigers "Leicester Tigers") at [Twickenham](/wiki/Twickenham_Stadium "Twickenham Stadium").

In November 2006 when it became apparent that Wales 'A' would not be able to face [Australia A national rugby union team](/wiki/Australia_A_national_rugby_union_team "Australia A national rugby union team") midweek during their [November tour](/wiki/2006_Wallabies_Spring_Tour "2006 Wallabies Spring Tour"), the Ospreys became the first Welsh region to play a major international side since regional rugby was introduced, a match they won 24–16.[[19]](#cite_note-wallabies-19) Following the win over the Wallabies, the term Ospreylia became ingrained in popular culture[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) with it being adopted by the region and its supporters as a description of the geographical area covered by the region, with supporters known as Ospreylians. [Peter Black](/wiki/Peter_Black_(Welsh_politician) "Peter Black (Welsh politician)"), Assembly Member for south west Wales has declared himself as [AM](/wiki/Member_of_the_National_Assembly_for_Wales "Member of the National Assembly for Wales") for Ospreylia on his own blog.[[23]](#cite_note-am-23)

On 23 February 2007, the Ospreys played their first 'A' team match, against Newport-Gwent Dragons 'A' at Bridgend, which was the first 'A' match fixture for any of the Welsh regional sides. They lost the match 22–10. They have since gone on to play Worcester 'A' in April, a game which they lost 24–23 to an injury-time penalty.

### 2008–2011

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ospreys_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2008–2011")]

In February 2008 [Andrew Hore](/wiki/Andrew_Hore_(rugby_coach) "Andrew Hore (rugby coach)") was appointed as the Ospreys as elite performance director. Previously Hore had been high performance manager with the [New Zealand Rugby Union](/wiki/New_Zealand_Rugby_Union "New Zealand Rugby Union").[[24]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-24)

A disappointing seventh-place finish in the Celtic League and a surprise Heineken Cup quarter-final exit to [Saracens F.C.](/wiki/Saracens_F.C. "Saracens F.C.") saw head coach [Lyn Jones](/wiki/Lyn_Jones "Lyn Jones") resign at the end of the 2007–08 season, on 16 May 2008 Assistant Coach Sean Holley and forwards coach Jonathan Humphreys would continue as part of a new management structure being overseen by Andrew Hore, until a replacement could be found.[[25]](#cite_note-25) [Scott Johnson](/wiki/Scott_Johnson_(rugby_coach) "Scott Johnson (rugby coach)"), the former head coach of the Welsh national team, and attack coach when Wales won the [Six Nations Championship](/wiki/Six_Nations_Championship "Six Nations Championship") [Grand Slam](/wiki/Grand_Slam_(rugby_union) "Grand Slam (rugby union)") in 2005 for the first time in 27 years, was announced as director of coaching at the Ospreys on 29 January 2009.

On 21 April 2009, the Ospreys had six players included in the [British & Irish Lions](/wiki/British_%26_Irish_Lions "British & Irish Lions")' squad for the 2009 South African tour: [Lee Byrne](/wiki/Lee_Byrne "Lee Byrne") (fullback), [Tommy Bowe](/wiki/Tommy_Bowe "Tommy Bowe") and [Shane Williams](/wiki/Shane_Williams "Shane Williams") (wings), [Mike Phillips](/wiki/Mike_Phillips_(rugby_union) "Mike Phillips (rugby union)") (scrum-half), [Alun Wyn Jones](/wiki/Alun_Wyn_Jones "Alun Wyn Jones") (lock) and [Adam Jones](/wiki/Adam_Jones_(rugby_union,_born_1981) "Adam Jones (rugby union, born 1981)") (prop). One notable omission however, was Wales and Ospreys captain [Ryan Jones](/wiki/Ryan_Jones "Ryan Jones"). The Ospreys announced the signing of former New Zealand [All Blacks](/wiki/All_Blacks "All Blacks") [captain](/wiki/Captain_(sports) "Captain (sports)") [Jerry Collins](/wiki/Jerry_Collins "Jerry Collins") on 9 May 2009.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Collins had been a stalwart for the All Blacks, since 2004 but retired from international rugby in 2008.

A 29–28 defeat by Biarritz on 10 April 2010 dashed the team's hopes of a first Heineken Cup semi-final appearance. A late Nicky Walker try in San Sebastien proved in vain.[[27]](#cite_note-27) However, on 29 May 2010, the Ospreys won the [2009–10 Celtic League](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_Celtic_League "2009–10 Celtic League") by defeating [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") in the final by 17–12 at the [RDS Arena](/wiki/RDS_Arena "RDS Arena") in Dublin.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Following a wide-ranging review, Andrew Hore, previously elite performance director at the Ospreys, became on 26 April 2011 chief operations officer at the region.[[24]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-24)

2010–11 would not see the region reach the heights of the previous one. Despite winning all their home pool games in the Heineken Cup, a failure to win on the road proved costly, with Munster and Toulon progressing instead. Another win for Munster at [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park") on 14 May 2011, ended the Ospreys Celtic League aspirations at the semi-final stage.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### 2012–2018

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ospreys_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2012–2018")]

Former player [Steve Tandy](/wiki/Steve_Tandy "Steve Tandy") was appointed as new Ospreys head coach on 15 February 2012 replacing Sean Holley in the role, as [Scott Johnson](/wiki/Scott_Johnson_(rugby_coach) "Scott Johnson (rugby coach)") also departed to take up a coaching role with [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_union_team "Scotland national rugby union team").[[30]](#cite_note-30) Both Holley and Johnson's final game as part of the coaching setup at the Liberty Stadium had been a 36–5 away loss to Biarritz, which marked the end of a Heineken Cup campaign that saw them again fail to win on their travels. New coach Tandy enjoyed a successful start to his tenure, winning eight of his first ten matches as full-time Head Coach, including winning the [2011–12 Pro12](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Pro12 "2011–12 Pro12") following a 31–30 win over Leinster at the RDS, thanks to a late [Shane Williams](/wiki/Shane_Williams "Shane Williams") try in his final match for the region 29–30 and Dan Biggar adding a difficult conversion for the win 31–30.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Ospreys reached 2 semi-finals in the subsequent 6 seasons, but not made the final.

Following a poor start to the 2017–2018 season, Tandy was sacked by the Ospreys.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Forwards coach [Allen Clarke](/wiki/Allen_Clarke_(rugby_union) "Allen Clarke (rugby union)") took over on an interim basis, before agreeing to a three-year deal as head coach.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### 2018–

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ospreys_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2018–")]

For the 2018–2019 season, along with new head coach Clarke, [Matt Sherratt](/wiki/Matt_Sherratt "Matt Sherratt") joined the coaching staff, replacing Gruff Rees.[[34]](#cite_note-34) The season also began with notable squad changes, with record points scorer [Dan Biggar](/wiki/Dan_Biggar "Dan Biggar") departing for [Northampton Saints](/wiki/Northampton_Saints "Northampton Saints"),[[35]](#cite_note-35) and stalwart [Paul James](/wiki/Paul_James_(rugby_union) "Paul James (rugby union)") retiring.[[36]](#cite_note-36) The Ospreys were boosted with the signings of Welsh internationals [George North](/wiki/George_North "George North"),[[37]](#cite_note-37) [Scott Williams](/wiki/Scott_Williams_(rugby_union) "Scott Williams (rugby union)"),[[38]](#cite_note-38) and [Aled Davies.](/wiki/Aled_Davies_(rugby_union) "Aled Davies (rugby union)")[[39]](#cite_note-39) The season saw a modest improvement for the Ospreys, winning 12 matches and losing 9, as well as beating west Wales rivals the Scarlets for a Champions Cup place.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The Ospreys endured an inauspicious 2019–2020 season, finishing bottom of Conference A of the Pro14, winning just two of their 15 matches.

The 2020–2021 season saw Allen Clarke replaced by former electrician [Toby Booth](/wiki/Toby_Booth "Toby Booth").[[41]](#cite_note-41) He succeeded in sparking the region back to life, including an away win at eventual champions Leinster [[42]](#cite_note-42) and a return to top tier European competition for the following season. In 2021–2022, the rebranded United Rugby Championship saw the Ospreys win the Welsh Shield, but struggle in the Champions Cup, losing all of their group games. They also narrowly missed out on the league play-offs, coming in ninth position. A home win over Munster heralded the arrival of outstanding back row forward Jac Morgan.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

The 2022–2023 season once again saw a mid-table finish in the United Rugby Championship. The Ospreys' European Champions Cup campaign started in ominous fashion, as a much-changed Leicester team defeated them at the Swansea.com stadium. As one of the lowest-seeded teams in the competition, the Ospreys would have to play the reigning English and French champions home and away in their group games. The defeat against Leicester was therefore followed by an away game in Montpellier. Against all expectations, and inspired by the arrival of master-tactician fly-half Owen Williams after the break-up of Worcester Warriors, the Ospreys beat Montpellier. That was followed by a win over Montpellier at home, and a titanic victory away at Leicester, which secured their place in the round of 16. Results elsewhere meant that they missed out on home advantage, and were ultimately not able to get past Saracens, bowing out of the competition at the round of 16 stage, but not without achieving some of the best results in Welsh regional rugby in years along the way.

In January 2024, the Ospreys management have stated they are looking at options for moving to a new ground. It will be a rugby home, suited to rugby supporters and not a shared football stadium like the Swansea.com.

## Home grounds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ospreys_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Home grounds")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Liberty_stadium_swansea.jpg/250px-Liberty_stadium_swansea.jpg)](/wiki/File:Liberty_stadium_swansea.jpg)

Liberty Stadium

In their first two seasons, the Ospreys shared their home games between [St Helen's](/wiki/St._Helen%27s_Rugby_and_Cricket_Ground "St. Helen's Rugby and Cricket Ground") (home ground of [Swansea RFC](/wiki/Swansea_RFC "Swansea RFC")) and [The Gnoll](/wiki/The_Gnoll "The Gnoll") (home ground of [Neath RFC](/wiki/Neath_RFC "Neath RFC")). Since their third season in 2005–06, they have played at the purpose-built 20,000 seat [Swansea.com Stadium](/wiki/Swansea.com_Stadium "Swansea.com Stadium") (originally the Liberty stadium) in [Landore](/wiki/Landore "Landore"), [Swansea](/wiki/Swansea "Swansea"), which is shared with [Swansea City](/wiki/Swansea_City_A.F.C. "Swansea City A.F.C."). The Swansea.com Stadium, with double the capacity of St Helen's, has seen a capacity crowd for the matches against international tourists [Australia 'A' team](/wiki/2006_Wallabies_Spring_Tour "2006 Wallabies Spring Tour") (1 November 2006[[47]](#cite_note-51)) and against local rivals the [Scarlets](/wiki/Scarlets "Scarlets").[[48]](#cite_note-52) On 12 November 2010 the team played their first competitive fixture at the [Brewery Field](/wiki/Brewery_Field "Brewery Field") home ground of [Bridgend Ravens](/wiki/Bridgend_Ravens "Bridgend Ravens") in the [Anglo-Welsh Cup](/wiki/Anglo-Welsh_Cup "Anglo-Welsh Cup").[[49]](#cite_note-53)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/245173-idl-zz-xx-dr-a-p2027-s3-p02_proposed_site_plan_option_a_phase_2_-_image_only.png/250px-245173-idl-zz-xx-dr-a-p2027-s3-p02_proposed_site_plan_option_a_phase_2_-_image_only.png)](/wiki/File:245173-idl-zz-xx-dr-a-p2027-s3-p02_proposed_site_plan_option_a_phase_2_-_image_only.png)

Full Phase 1 and Phase 2 plan for St Helens Rugby and Cricket Ground reconstruction

In July 2024, it was announced that the Ospreys would leave the [Swansea.com Stadium](/wiki/Swansea.com_Stadium "Swansea.com Stadium") after 20 years and return to play in the newly redeveloped [St Helen's Rugby and Cricket Ground](/wiki/St_Helen%27s_Rugby_and_Cricket_Ground "St Helen's Rugby and Cricket Ground"). As part of the reconfiguration of the ground, it was also announced that the Swansea Cricket Club would leave the home they had occupied since 1875.[[50]](#cite_note-54)[[51]](#cite_note-55) However, it was announced on 12 August 2025 that the Ospreys would play all of their home games at the Brewery Field for the 2025–26 season. This was done to allow redevelopment work to be completed at St Helen's, due to be completed by the start of the following season.[[52]](#cite_note-56)

## Kit suppliers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ospreys_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Kit suppliers")]

From their foundation to the 2013–14 season, the Ospreys kit suppliers were [Kooga](/wiki/Kooga "Kooga"). Between the seasons 2014–2017, [BLK](/wiki/Kooga "Kooga") supplied the Ospreys kits.[[53]](#cite_note-57) [Canterbury](/wiki/Canterbury_of_New_Zealand "Canterbury of New Zealand") supplied the kits for the 2018–2021 seasons. Starting for the 2021/22 season, the Ospreys would form a kit partnership with [Umbro](/wiki/Umbro "Umbro").[[54]](#cite_note-58) This lasted until 2023 as in June of the same year, the Ospreys announced that they had signed a multi-year partnership with sportswear manufacturer [Macron](/wiki/Macron_(sportswear) "Macron (sportswear)").[[55]](#cite_note-59)

## ERC Elite Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ospreys_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: ERC Elite Awards")]

During the 10th anniversary season of the Heineken Cup, [ERC](/wiki/European_Rugby_Cup "European Rugby Cup"), the tournament organisers, introduced the ERC Elite Awards to recognise players and teams who have made outstanding contributions to the tournament.

Ospreys were awarded the ERC team award for playing 50 games.[[62]](#cite_note-68)

Ospreys players who have been awarded 50 tournament caps are:[[63]](#cite_note-69)

* [Ian Gough](/wiki/Ian_Gough "Ian Gough")
* [Adam Jones](/wiki/Adam_Jones_(rugby_union,_born_1981) "Adam Jones (rugby union, born 1981)")
* [Duncan Jones](/wiki/Duncan_Jones_(rugby_union) "Duncan Jones (rugby union)")
* [Shane Williams](/wiki/Shane_Williams "Shane Williams")
* [Jonathan Thomas](/wiki/Jonathan_Thomas "Jonathan Thomas")
* [Sonny Parker](/wiki/Sonny_Parker_(rugby_union) "Sonny Parker (rugby union)")
